content:
You are the Accumulator. Given one paper (markdown + optionally a cloned
GitHub repo), do TWO things:
  1. Assign the paper to ONE of the 7 ML domains below.
  2. Extract 1–3 distinct main contributions as seeds.

Each seed captures (a) the underlying problem the paper addresses, and
(b) the method that solves it, including what is special / novel about
the approach. Avoid splitting one idea into multiple sub-seeds; prefer
fewer, higher-quality seeds.

## Domains (pick exactly one)
- LLM_NLP    : language models, NLP, text-only agents
- MULTIMODAL : cross-modality (text+image, text+audio, etc.).
               Vision-Language Models (VLM, e.g., CLIP / LLaVA / GPT-4V)
               belong HERE, not under CV.
- CV         : visual perception (classification, detection, segmentation,
               video understanding) AND image/video generation
               (diffusion, GAN, VAE) when the contribution is the
               generative mechanism on visual data.
- RL         : reinforcement learning algorithms, policies, environments;
               RLHF when the contribution is the RL algorithm itself.
- IR_REC     : information retrieval, recommender systems, search.
- SPEECH     : speech / audio (TTS, ASR, audio language models).
- ROBOTICS   : embodied agents, manipulation, planning in physical world.
               If a paper proposes an embodied policy, prefer ROBOTICS
               over RL.

## Workspace contract
Paper directory:    /work/2511.06857
paper.md            Pre-converted markdown of the PDF.
repo/               Will exist if you `git clone` it. Otherwise unavailable.

## Tools
- Read       : view paper.md and any file under the paper directory
- Grep       : search inside paper.md or the cloned repo
- Bash       : `git clone <url> /work/2511.06857/repo` (only if you've found a
               *clearly official* GitHub URL in the paper);
               also general filesystem exploration: tree / ls / find / wc

## Output (terminate the run)
When you have finished, stop calling tools and reply with a SINGLE JSON
object inside a ```json fenced block. No extra prose:
  ```json
  {"title": "...", "domain": "<one of 7>", "seeds": [{"problem": "...", "method": "..."}, ...]}
  ```
- title  = the paper's actual title (NOT a venue label or page header)
- domain = exactly one of LLM_NLP / MULTIMODAL / CV / RL / IR_REC / SPEECH / ROBOTICS
- seeds  = 1–3 entries, or empty list if extraction failed

## Default flow
1. Read paper.md end-to-end. Identify the paper's true title (often the
   first H1, but PDF→md sometimes places venue labels / preprint headers
   above it — pick the actual title, not the noise). For longer papers
   a single Read may not cover everything; continue with Read(offset=...)
   until you actually understand the methodology and experiments
   sections, not just the abstract and introduction.
2. Decide the domain from the 7 above. Use the tie-breakers in the list
   when a paper sits between two.
3. Look for an official GitHub URL (abstract, footnote 1, "Code:" line, etc.).
   If you find one and it is unambiguously the paper's repo, clone it.
   If you are unsure, do NOT clone — proceed paper-only.
4. (If repo cloned) Use Bash / Grep to locate where the main contribution
   is implemented. Read enough to confirm what's special.
5. Draft 1–3 candidate seeds in your reasoning. Each seed:
     - problem: 1–2 sentences. The challenge the paper addresses.
     - method: 3–5 sentences. The solution AND what is special about it.
6. BEFORE emitting the final JSON, verify each candidate seed against
   the paper. For each seed:
     - Identify 2-3 specific claims your `method` text makes
       (mechanism name, key design choice, quantitative finding).
     - Use Grep on paper.md to locate where each claim is supported,
       and Read(offset=...) to inspect the surrounding context if the
       Grep snippet is insufficient. (Use repo Grep/Read if cloned.)
     - Revise the seed text to match the paper. If a claim cannot be
       grounded after re-reading, REMOVE that claim or drop the seed
       entirely.
   This step is MANDATORY: do not skip it because a seed feels right.
7. Stop calling tools and emit the final JSON object described under
   "## Output". This terminates the run.

Budget: 100 tool calls. Plan first, then act.

## Paper
Paper directory: /work/2511.06857

Begin by reading paper.md.

1	## Ambiguity-aware Truncated Flow Matching for Ambiguous Medical Image Segmentation
     2	
     3	Fanding Li 1 , Xiangyu Li 1 ∗ , Xianghe Su 1 , Xingyu Qiu 1 , Suyu Dong 2 , Wei Wang 3 , Kuanquan Wang 1 , Gongning Luo 1 , Shuo Li 4,5
     4	
     5	1 Faculty of Computing, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin, China 2 College of Computer and Control Engineering, Northeast Forestry University, Harbin, China 3 Faculty of Computing, Harbin Institute of Technology, Shenzhen, China
     6	
     7	4 Department of Computer and Data Science, Case Western Reserve University, Cleveland, Ohio 44106, United States
     8	
     9	5 Department of Biomedical Engineering, Case Western Reserve University, Cleveland, Ohio 44106, United States lixiangyu@hit.edu.cn
    10	
    11	## Abstract
    12	
    13	A simultaneous enhancement of accuracy and diversity of predictions remains a challenge in ambiguous medical image segmentation (AMIS) due to the inherent trade-offs. While truncated diffusion probabilistic models (TDPMs) hold strong potential with a paradigm optimization, existing TDPMs suffer from entangled accuracy and diversity of predictions with insufficient fidelity and plausibility. To address the aforementioned challenges, we propose Ambiguity-aware Truncated Flow Matching (ATFM), which introduces a novel inference paradigm and dedicated model components. Firstly, we propose Data-Hierarchical Inference, a redefinition of AMISspecific inference paradigm, which enhances accuracy and diversity at data-distribution and data-sample level, respectively, for an effective disentanglement. Secondly, Gaussian Truncation Representation (GTR) is introduced to enhance both fidelity of predictions and reliability of truncation distribution, by explicitly modeling it as a Gaussian distribution at 𝑇 trunc instead of using sampling-based approximations. Thirdly, Segmentation Flow Matching (SFM) is proposed to enhance the plausibility of diverse predictions by extending semantic-aware flow transformation in Flow Matching (FM). Comprehensive evaluations on LIDC and ISIC3 datasets demonstrate that ATFM outperforms SOTA methods and simultaneously achieves a more efficient inference. ATFM improves GED and HM-IoU by up to 12% and 7 . 3% compared to advanced methods.
    14	
    15	## Code -
    16	
    17	https://github.com/PerceptionComputingLab/ATFM Extended version -This is the extended version of AAAI paper with full appendixes
    18	
    19	## Introduction
    20	
    21	Generating a series of predictions with high accuracy and diversity to estimate the distribution of annotation space is of significant importance in ambiguous medical image segmentation (AMIS) (Chavhan et al. 2008; Hong et al. 2021). High diversity reflects the inherent ambiguity present in medical images and high accuracy is critical for supporting dependable clinical decision-making, making both essential components of a reliable AMIS framework (Baumgartner et al. 2019; Zhang et al. 2022).
    22	
    23	∗ Corresponding Author
    24	
    25	Copyright © 2026, Association for the Advancement of Artificial Intelligence (www.aaai.org). All rights reserved.
    26	
    27	Figure 1: (a) Traditional TDPMs face the challenges of low fidelity and plausibility of predictions by improving diversity at the expense of accuracy. (b) The proposed ATFM enhances fidelity and plausibility of predictions by assigning distinct inference goals into two stages.
    28	
    29	<!-- image -->
    30	
    31	However, simultaneously improving prediction accuracy and diversity remains challenging in AMIS due to the inherent trade-off between these objectives in existing methods. Stochastic approaches (Baumgartner et al. 2019; Rahman et al. 2023) enhance diversity at the expense of the more important accuracy, yielding low-confidence diagnoses. Zhang et al. (Zhang et al. 2022) are able to regulate this trade-off but cannot enhance both properties simultaneously. Multi-rateraware techniques (Zepf et al. 2023; Ji et al. 2021) improve accuracy and diversity by modeling annotators' labeling styles, yet their annotator-centric design inherently suppresses lowfrequency modes, degrading segmentation quality. Broader application of these methods is still constrained by the inherent trade-off between entangled accuracy and diversity among predictions.
    32	
    33	Truncated Diffusion Probabilistic Models (TDPMs) (Zheng et al. 2022) have shown great potential in simul- taneously improving accuracy and diversity thanks to the inference paradigm shift. TDPMs are proposed to inference within fewer steps by leveraging an auxiliary network to model the distribution at a predefined truncation point, shifting the original inference for acceleration. TDPMs are widely used in multiple areas such as high-resolution MRSI generating (Dong et al. 2025), autonomous driving (Liao et al. 2025), remote sensing (He et al. 2023), demonstrating superiority in both effectiveness and efficiency of inference.
    34	
    35	However, as Fig. 1(a), directly applying TDPMs to achieve synergistic optimization of prediction accuracy and diversity in AMIS remains challenging due to: (1) The uniform inference objective across all stages in conventional TDPMs inherently constrains their capacity for simultaneous accuracy and diversity improvement. Vanilla TDPMs adopt a two-stage inference process with one objective mainly for acceleration. However, the deterministic and ambiguous components remain entangled throughout the inference process, making it difficult to achieve a simultaneous enhancement of both accuracy and diversity. (2) Sub-optimal approximation of the underlying distribution at the truncation point fundamentally compromises prediction fidelity. Distribution at 𝑇 trunc is estimated by drawing samples from adversarial networks rather than explicitly modeling in vanilla TDPMs, leading to degraded fidelity caused by inconsistent predictions and omission of low-frequency modes (clinically plausible yet rare). (3) The absence of semantic guidance following truncation in conventional TDPMs adversely affects the plausibility of generated predictions. Vanilla TDPMs rely on a vanilla diffusion process guided solely by generation quality after 𝑇 trunc. In AMIS tasks, this final stage lacks explicit semantic constraints for segmentation, which, although enhancing diversity, significantly compromises the more important accuracy and plausibility.
    36	
    37	In this work, we propose the Ambiguity-aware Truncated Flow Matching (ATFM) to achieve a synergistic enhancement of accuracy and diversity of predictions in AMIS tasks, supported by three designs (as Fig. 1(b)): (1) We propose Data-Hierarchical Inference, an innovative AMIS-specific inference paradigm redefinition inspired by TDPMs, where stochasticity during diffusion is marginalized for an effective disentanglement of accuracy and diversity. Specifically, ATFMperforms truncated steps at the data distribution level to prioritize accuracy with diversity marginalized, whereas the final diffusion stage operates at the data sample level to enhance diversity without sacrificing accuracy. (2) We propose Gaussian Truncation Representation (GTR), which explicitly models the Gaussian latent distribution at truncation point to enhance prediction fidelity. While traditional TDPMs approximate the implicit distribution via adversarial sampling, GTR encodes image-level semantic features to logit distribution, thereby directly modeling the reliable distribution at the truncation point. This design preserves low-frequency modes and improves consistency, leading to predictions with higher fidelity in AMIS tasks. (3) We propose Segmentation Flow Matching (SFM), which introduces semantic-aware flow transformation to increase diversity and enhance plausibility simultaneously. While traditional TDPMs focus solely on generative quality after truncated steps, the proposed SFM
    38	
    39	leverages Flow Matching (FM) to overcome Gaussian limitations that disrupt fine-grained predictions and incorporates explicit semantic consistency modeling to ensure the plausibility of segmentation predictions in AMIS tasks.
    40	
    41	In summary, our main contributions are as follows:
    42	
    43	- We propose ATFM with Data-Hierarchical Inference redefining a more suitable inference paradigm for AMIS for the first time, where Data-Hierarchical Inference effectively decouples accuracy and diversity by marginalizing the stochasticity during diffusion process, thereby enabling a simultaneous improvement of both.
    44	- The proposed GTR in ATFM pioneers explicitly modeling a Gaussian distribution at truncation point, which effectively preserves low-frequency modes and ensures sample consistency, thereby significantly improving prediction fidelity to ground truth distribution and reliability of the distribution at truncation point.
    45	- The proposed SFM in ATFM pioneers semantic-aware flow transformation by modeling semantic consistency at each timestep, thereby enhancing plausibility while emphasizing sample-wise diversity, and is built upon the Flow Matching (FM) process that inherently avoids disturbance from Gaussian constraints.
    46	- Acomprehensive evaluation on the LIDC and ISIC3 subset datasets demonstrates that proposed ATFM significantly improves the SOTA methods and simultaneously achieves a more efficient inference.
    47	
    48	## Related Work
    49	
    50	## Ambiguous Medical Image Segmentation
    51	
    52	Existing AMIS approaches fall into four main paradigms: model ensemble, multi-head frameworks, conditional variational autoencoder (cVAE)-based models, and diffusionbased models. All face a fundamental trade-off between prediction accuracy and sample diversity (Zhang et al. 2022).
    53	
    54	Modelensemble(Monteiroetal.2020;Lipmanetal.2023) and multi-head models (Ho, Jain, and Abbeel 2020; Kohl et al. 2018) generate multiple predictions via diverse architectures or output heads but do not change the original inference process. Consequently, prediction quality heavily depends on model selection, limiting simultaneous improvement of accuracy and diversity.
    55	
    56	CVAE-based (Baumgartner et al. 2019; Kohl et al. 2019) and diffusion-based methods (Rahman et al. 2023; Zbinden et al. 2023) inject stochasticity to enhance diversity, yet both follow a one-stage inference paradigm that entangles accuracy and diversity optimization. This leads to inherent conflicts where gains in diversity often reduce accuracy.
    57	
    58	To address the challenges, in the proposed ATFM, we introduce Data-Hierarchical Inference to redefine the inference paradigm for AMIS inspired by TDPMs. Specifically, a principled decoupling of the two objectives is achieved across different data hierarchies, where accuracy is enhanced at distributional level and diversity is promoted at sample level.
    59	
    60	## Truncated Diffusion Probabilistic Models
    61	
    62	Truncated Diffusion Probabilistic Models (TDPMs) accelerate inference by truncating the diffusion process at 𝑇 trunc ≪
    63	
    64	Figure 2: The proposed ATFM addresses the challenge of a synergistic optimization by boosting accuracy at distribution level and diversity at sample level within Data-Hierarchical Inference (Sec. 3.1) while enhancing fidelity and plausibility with GTR (Sec. 3.2) and SFM (Sec. 3.3), respectively.
    65	
    66	<!-- image -->
    67	
    68	Figure 3: Data-Hierarchical Inference disentangles accuracy and diversity by marginalizing stochasticity during diffusion with a data-distribution level supervision.
    69

    71	
    72	𝑇 , splitting inference into estimating the distribution at 𝑇 trunc and reverse diffusion thereafter. Existing TDPMs approximate the distribution at 𝑇 trunc via adversarial sampling or perturbations (Zheng et al. 2022; Dong et al. 2025), improving speed but without redefining the inference paradigm. Consequently, they lack explicit modeling and semantic supervision at 𝑇 trunc, limiting performance on AMIS tasks.
    73	
    74	The proposed ATFM addresses these gaps by introducing Data-Hierarchical Inference, which marginalizes stochasticity before truncation to disentangle and enhance both accuracy and diversity. Consequently, ATFM explicitly models a Gaussian distribution at 𝑇 trunc for high-fidelity sampling and applies semantic supervision after truncation to improve plausibility, making ATFM a tailored solution for AMIS.
    75	
    76	## Methods
    77	
    78	The proposed ATFM (Fig. 2) redefines the inference paradigm for disentanglement and simultaneous enhance- ment of accuracy and diversity by marginalizing stochasticity during truncation, forming an AMIS-specific defining solution. ( Data-Hierarchical Inference, Sec. 3.1 ). Specifically, ATFM firstly improves prediction fidelity and truncation distribution reliability by explicitly modeling the Gaussian distribution at truncation point, thereby preserving lowfrequency modes often missed by sampling-based approximation and ensuring consistency across predictions ( GTR, Sec. 3.2 ). Secondly, ATFM extends semantic-aware flow transformation by modeling semantic consistency among labels, predictions, and intermediate states during flow matching (FM), thereby enhancing prediction plausibility while promoting diversity. Additionally, Gaussian constraints are avoided for fine-grained predictions by FM ( SFM, Sec. 3.3 ).
    79	
    80	## Data-Hierarchical Inference Enables Principled Disentanglement and Joint Enhancement of Accuracy and Diversity
    81	
    82	The proposed Data-Hierarchical Inference forms disentanglement and simultaneous enhancement of prediction accuracy and diversity by marginalizing the stochasticity during diffusion, which introduces a redefinition of AMIS-specific inference paradigm. While preserving the efficiency gains from truncation, Data-Hierarchical Inference introduces a principled separation between distribution-level and samplelevel inference, dedicated to optimizing overall accuracy and prediction diversity, respectively.
    83	
    84	Specifically, the overall inference process firstly focuses on improving accuracy by supervising an accurate explicit distribution at 𝑇 trunc as eq. 1, and then enhances diversity by generating varied samples from this distribution after 𝑇 trunc as eq. 2, ensuring each prediction remains both distinct and consistent with the underlying semantics.

    93	
    94	As illustrated in Fig. 3 and eq. 1, Data-Hierarchical Inference fundamentally redefines the inference paradigm by marginalizing stochasticity during truncation to achieve a principled disentanglement of accuracy and diversity. This paradigm optimization enables an improvement in accuracy without compromising diversity. Sample-level diffusion builds upon the globally aligned explicit distribution at 𝑇 trunc , leading to a unified and robust enhancement of both prediction fidelity and diversity.
    95	
    96	Data-Hierarchical Inference inherently addresses the core challenges of AMIS by reconciling high prediction accuracy with plausible diversity. Through principled disentanglement of accuracy and diversity, Data-Hierarchical Inference establishes a robust and efficient solution that redefines the inference paradigm and application of TDPMs in AMIS.

   166	## Experiments
   167	
   168	## Experimental Setup
   169	
   170	Datasets. In our experiments, we applied two public datasets for ambiguous medical image segmentation: LIDCIDRI (Kalpathy-Cramer et al. 2016) and ISIC3 subset (Codella et al. 2019; Zepf et al. 2023). The LIDC-IDRI dataset consists of lung CT scans with multiple expertannotated lesion segmentations, highlighting diagnostic ambiguities. Following preprocessing as described in (Kohl et al. 2018, 2019), the dataset includes 15,096 slices, each with four corresponding segmentation labels. The ISIC3 dataset provides dermoscopic images of skin lesions with annotations for lesion boundaries. Using the preprocessed ISIC3 subset from (Zepf et al. 2023), we work with 300 images, each featuring exactly three annotations.

For individual segmentation accuracy, we utilize the Maximum Dice Matching (MDM) (Rahmanet al. 2023) to evaluate the best Dice scores between each prediction result and each ground truth. We denote the metrics with subscript n to represent the metrics calculated using n samples. Results are reported as mean ± standard deviation over five independent runs.

   200	## Comparison with SOTAs for Performance Superiority.
   201	
   202	Quantitative Evaluation. Table 1 and Table 2 report the quantitative results on the LIDC and ISIC3 datasets, respectively. Across all key metrics-GED (for diversity), HM-IoU (for sample fidelity), and MDM (for individual accuracy)-ATFM consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods. Notably, ATFM achieves an 11.5% improvement on GED100 in LIDC, along with consistent gains in GED16 and GED32 compared with the runner-up method, highlighting its ability to better capture the underlying segmentation label distribution. A minimum of 7.3% improvement in HM-IoU32 further demonstrates the superior fidelity of the generated samples. ATFM also leads in the MDM32 metric, validating its accuracy at the individual prediction level. Similar trends are observed on ISIC3, where ATFM outperforms the runnerup method by 12% in GED while also achieving top-tier results in HM-IoU and MDM, demonstrating comprehensive improvements in diversity, fidelity, and accuracy. These results showcase the effectiveness of the Data-Hierarchical Inference framework of ATFM, which explicitly models intermediate distributions and enforces semantic-aware flow transformations to enhance accuracy and diversity in AMIS.

   216	## Conclusion
   217	
   218	In this work, we proposed Ambiguity-aware Truncated Flow Matching (ATFM), addressing the challenge of jointly improving prediction accuracy and diversity via a novel inference paradigm and dedicated model components, tailored to the demands of AMIS tasks. Specifically, we firstly proposed Data-Hierarchical Inference, redefining a novel inference paradigm that disentangles prediction accuracy and diversity, which supervises a distribution for accuracy at 𝑇 trunc by marginalizing stochasticity and promoting diversity through controlled sampling in the following timesteps. Onthis foundation, we designed two key modules for ATFM: GTR, which explicitly models the Gaussian distribution at truncation point to ensure prediction fidelity and truncation distribution reliability for overall accuracy; and SFM, which extends semantic-aware flow transformation to model semantic consistency across predictions, annotations and intermediate states for enhancing prediction plausibility while promoting diversity. Experimental results on two public datasets showed that the proposed ATFM outperforms SOTA methods across all metrics and offers a more efficient inference process simultaneously. ATFM offers a versatile and reliable solution for AMIS across a broader range of scenarios through multifaceted analysis and outstanding performance.

   230	## Appendix 2: Potential Underlying Mechanism of Truncated Diffusion Probabilistic Models
   231	
   232	Truncated Diffusion Probabilistic Models (TDPMs) demonstrate their potential through the introduction of an inference paradigm shift-a concept that optimizes the inference trajectory between start point and a predefined truncation timestep 𝑇 trunc of diffusion models. Specifically, TDPMs retain the original starting point (random noise) and truncation point ( 𝑋 𝑇 trunc ) of the diffusion process, while strategically replacing the intermediate inference steps between 𝑡 = 0 and 𝑡 = 𝑇 trunc with a learned truncation module (usually adversarial sampling). This substitution constitutes a paradigm shift, proving that the intermediate reasoning path of diffusion models is not rigid but can be restructured. ( As the comparison illustrated in green boxes in Fig. 7(a) and Fig. 7(b) )
   233	
   234	Although the primary goal of TDPMs is to accelerate inference, their architecture implicitly reveals a structural flexibility in diffusion-based generative models. By truncating and replacing part of the denoising trajectory, TDPMs validate the feasibility of modeling this process differently. This foundational insight is particularly valuable for ambiguous medical image segmentation (AMIS), where jointly improving prediction accuracy and diversity remains a key challenge. Thus, TDPMs not only serve as a faster alternative, but also inspire a reconsideration of how inference is formulated in diffusion frameworks especially for AMIS tasks.
   235	
   236	## Appendix 3: Superior Mechanism of the proposed Ambiguity-aware Truncated Flow Matching for AMIS Inspired by TDPMs
   237	
   238	Inspired by the inference paradigm shift introduced by TDPMs, the proposed ATFM redefines an AMIS-specific inference paradigm. ATFM further reformulates not only the overall inference paradigm, but the detailed truncation process and the remaining diffusion steps as well to address the inherent challenges of AMIS. ( as the improvement illustrated in Fig. 7(b) and Fig. 7(c) )
   239	
   240	Firstly, the overall inference paradigm is redefined through the proposed Data-Hierarchical Inference, which assigns distinct objectives to different stages. The truncation step focuses on enhancing overall accuracy at the distribution level without compromising diversity, while the subsequent diffusion stage promotes sample-level diversity based on the high-fidelity distribution obtained earlier. This overall design enables a principled disentanglement and simultaneous improvement of both accuracy and diversity. ( overall comparison between Fig. 7(b) and Fig. 7(c) )
   241	
   242	Secondly, the truncation stage is redefined via the proposed Gaussian Truncation Representation (GTR), which explicitly models the truncation point as a learnable Gaussian distribution with parameterized mean and covariance. This replaces the sampling-based approximation in TDPMs with a stable and semantically meaningful representation. As a result, it enhances prediction fidelity and provides a reliable initialization for subsequent inference. ( comparison illustrated in green boxes between Fig. 7(b) and Fig. 7(c) )
   243	
   244	Thirdly, the post-truncation diffusion stage is redefined through Segmentation Flow Matching (SFM), which supervises semantic consistency between intermediate states, predictions, and ground truths across timesteps. This transforms the diffusion process from unconstrained denoising into a semantics-aware transformation. FM further avoids disturbance from Gaussian constraints. SFM ensures prediction plausibility while capturing diverse sample-level variations in a structured manner. ( comparison illustrated in purple boxes between Fig. 7(b) and Fig. 7(c) )
   245	
   246	Fig. 8 further analyzes the three main limitations of vanilla TDPMsin the context of AMIS requirements, along with the corresponding solutions introduced by ATFM, which fundamentally explains the reason behind the differences between the two inference paradigms and highlights the advantages of ATFM. Specifically, these limitations relate to the overall inference paradigm, the truncation process, and the post-truncation diffusion stage. In response, ATFM addresses these issues through the proposed Data-Hierarchical Inference, Gaussian Truncation Representation, and Semantic Flow Matching, respectively.

   310	## Appendix 6: Detailed Network Structures Structure of Network in GTR.
   311	
   312	The network in GTR adopts a standard encoder-decoder architecture with 4 resolution levels. The encoder consists of 4 convolutional blocks with increasing filter sizes of 32, 64, 128, and 192. Each block applies two consecutive 3 × 3 convolutions followed by ReLU activations and downsampling via 2 × 2 max pooling. The decoder mirrors the encoder using transposed convolutions for upsampling and skip connections from corresponding encoder layers. On top of the final feature map, three separate 1 × 1 convolutional layers are applied to estimate the mean 𝜇 , and the variance Σ = 𝐷𝐷 𝑇 + 𝐿 for a multivariate Gaussian distribution with rank 𝑟 = 10, which parameterizes the explicit Gaussian distribution at 𝑇 trunc .
   313	
   314	## Structure of ST-Net in SFM.
   315	
   316	ST-Net is a temporal-conditional U-Net specifically designed as the backbone of SFM in the proposed ATFM. The network adopts a four-level encoder-decoder structure with skip connections, comprising 15 residual blocks in total. Each resolution stage includes group normalization and Swish activation, followed by linear attention blocks at all levels and full self-attention at the bottleneck layer to capture both local and global dependencies.
   317	
   318	Temporal information is injected into the network via sinusoidal time-step embeddings, which are processed through a two-layer MLP and fused into each residual block. Optional self-conditioning is supported to enhance performance. Spatial downsampling and upsampling are implemented using strided and transposed convolutions, respectively, maintaining the spatial structure of segmentation maps.
   319	
   320	This design enables expressive and time-aware feature representation, which is essential for accurately modeling the semantic transformation process in diffusion-based ambiguous image segmentation.

The optimal one-to-one assignment 𝜋 ∗ is then obtained using the Hungarian algorithm to maximize the total IoU. 13:A simultaneous enhancement of accuracy and diversity of predictions remains a challenge in ambiguous medical image segmentation (AMIS) due to the inherent trade-offs. While truncated diffusion probabilistic models (TDPMs) hold strong potential with a paradigm optimization, existing TDPMs suffer from entangled accuracy and diversity of predictions with insufficient fidelity and plausibility. To address the aforementioned challenges, we propose Ambiguity-aware Truncated Flow Matching (ATFM), which introduces a novel inference paradigm and dedicated model components. Firstly, we propose Data-Hierarchical Inference, a redefinition of AMISspecific inference paradigm, which enhances accuracy and diversity at data-distribution and data-sample level, respectively, for an effective disentanglement. Secondly, Gaussian Truncation Representation (GTR) is introduced to enhance both fidelity of predictions and reliability of truncation distribution, by explicitly modeling it as a Gaussian distribution at 𝑇 trunc instead of using sampling-based approximations. Thirdly, Segmentation Flow Matching (SFM) is proposed to enhance the plausibility of diverse predictions by extending semantic-aware flow transformation in Flow Matching (FM). Comprehensive evaluations on LIDC and ISIC3 datasets demonstrate that ATFM outperforms SOTA methods and simultaneously achieves a more efficient inference. ATFM improves GED and HM-IoU by up to 12% and 7 . 3% compared to advanced methods.
37:In this work, we propose the Ambiguity-aware Truncated Flow Matching (ATFM) to achieve a synergistic enhancement of accuracy and diversity of predictions in AMIS tasks, supported by three designs (as Fig. 1(b)): (1) We propose Data-Hierarchical Inference, an innovative AMIS-specific inference paradigm redefinition inspired by TDPMs, where stochasticity during diffusion is marginalized for an effective disentanglement of accuracy and diversity. Specifically, ATFMperforms truncated steps at the data distribution level to prioritize accuracy with diversity marginalized, whereas the final diffusion stage operates at the data sample level to enhance diversity without sacrificing accuracy. (2) We propose Gaussian Truncation Representation (GTR), which explicitly models the Gaussian latent distribution at truncation point to enhance prediction fidelity. While traditional TDPMs approximate the implicit distribution via adversarial sampling, GTR encodes image-level semantic features to logit distribution, thereby directly modeling the reliable distribution at the truncation point. This design preserves low-frequency modes and improves consistency, leading to predictions with higher fidelity in AMIS tasks. (3) We propose Segmentation Flow Matching (SFM), which introduces semantic-aware flow transformation to increase diversity and enhance plausibility simultaneously. While traditional TDPMs focus solely on generative quality after truncated steps, the proposed SFM
43:- We propose ATFM with Data-Hierarchical Inference redefining a more suitable inference paradigm for AMIS for the first time, where Data-Hierarchical Inference effectively decouples accuracy and diversity by marginalizing the stochasticity during diffusion process, thereby enabling a simultaneous improvement of both.
58:To address the challenges, in the proposed ATFM, we introduce Data-Hierarchical Inference to redefine the inference paradigm for AMIS inspired by TDPMs. Specifically, a principled decoupling of the two objectives is achieved across different data hierarchies, where accuracy is enhanced at distributional level and diversity is promoted at sample level.
64:Figure 2: The proposed ATFM addresses the challenge of a synergistic optimization by boosting accuracy at distribution level and diversity at sample level within Data-Hierarchical Inference (Sec. 3.1) while enhancing fidelity and plausibility with GTR (Sec. 3.2) and SFM (Sec. 3.3), respectively.
68:Figure 3: Data-Hierarchical Inference disentangles accuracy and diversity by marginalizing stochasticity during diffusion with a data-distribution level supervision.
74:The proposed ATFM addresses these gaps by introducing Data-Hierarchical Inference, which marginalizes stochasticity before truncation to disentangle and enhance both accuracy and diversity. Consequently, ATFM explicitly models a Gaussian distribution at 𝑇 trunc for high-fidelity sampling and applies semantic supervision after truncation to improve plausibility, making ATFM a tailored solution for AMIS.
78:The proposed ATFM (Fig. 2) redefines the inference paradigm for disentanglement and simultaneous enhance- ment of accuracy and diversity by marginalizing stochasticity during truncation, forming an AMIS-specific defining solution. ( Data-Hierarchical Inference, Sec. 3.1 ). Specifically, ATFM firstly improves prediction fidelity and truncation distribution reliability by explicitly modeling the Gaussian distribution at truncation point, thereby preserving lowfrequency modes often missed by sampling-based approximation and ensuring consistency across predictions ( GTR, Sec. 3.2 ). Secondly, ATFM extends semantic-aware flow transformation by modeling semantic consistency among labels, predictions, and intermediate states during flow matching (FM), thereby enhancing prediction plausibility while promoting diversity. Additionally, Gaussian constraints are avoided for fine-grained predictions by FM ( SFM, Sec. 3.3 ).
80:## Data-Hierarchical Inference Enables Principled Disentanglement and Joint Enhancement of Accuracy and Diversity
82:The proposed Data-Hierarchical Inference forms disentanglement and simultaneous enhancement of prediction accuracy and diversity by marginalizing the stochasticity during diffusion, which introduces a redefinition of AMIS-specific inference paradigm. While preserving the efficiency gains from truncation, Data-Hierarchical Inference introduces a principled separation between distribution-level and samplelevel inference, dedicated to optimizing overall accuracy and prediction diversity, respectively.
92:where 𝑃 denotes the explicit intermediate distribution estimated by our Data-Hierarchical Inference at 𝑇 trunc, and 𝑄 represents the corresponding distribution derived from ground truths. 𝑠 𝑖 denote latent samples, pred 𝑖 are the corresponding predictions, and gt 𝑖 represent the ground truths.
94:As illustrated in Fig. 3 and eq. 1, Data-Hierarchical Inference fundamentally redefines the inference paradigm by marginalizing stochasticity during truncation to achieve a principled disentanglement of accuracy and diversity. This paradigm optimization enables an improvement in accuracy without compromising diversity. Sample-level diffusion builds upon the globally aligned explicit distribution at 𝑇 trunc , leading to a unified and robust enhancement of both prediction fidelity and diversity.
96:Data-Hierarchical Inference inherently addresses the core challenges of AMIS by reconciling high prediction accuracy with plausible diversity. Through principled disentanglement of accuracy and diversity, Data-Hierarchical Inference establishes a robust and efficient solution that redefines the inference paradigm and application of TDPMs in AMIS.
98:Summarized Advantage : Data-Hierarchical Inference, pioneers disentangling accuracy and diversity for better meeting with requirements of AMIS, by marginalizing diversity and promoting it through controlled sampling within an inference paradigm redefinition in two consecutive stages.
202:Quantitative Evaluation. Table 1 and Table 2 report the quantitative results on the LIDC and ISIC3 datasets, respectively. Across all key metrics-GED (for diversity), HM-IoU (for sample fidelity), and MDM (for individual accuracy)-ATFM consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods. Notably, ATFM achieves an 11.5% improvement on GED100 in LIDC, along with consistent gains in GED16 and GED32 compared with the runner-up method, highlighting its ability to better capture the underlying segmentation label distribution. A minimum of 7.3% improvement in HM-IoU32 further demonstrates the superior fidelity of the generated samples. ATFM also leads in the MDM32 metric, validating its accuracy at the individual prediction level. Similar trends are observed on ISIC3, where ATFM outperforms the runnerup method by 12% in GED while also achieving top-tier results in HM-IoU and MDM, demonstrating comprehensive improvements in diversity, fidelity, and accuracy. These results showcase the effectiveness of the Data-Hierarchical Inference framework of ATFM, which explicitly models intermediate distributions and enforces semantic-aware flow transformations to enhance accuracy and diversity in AMIS.
218:In this work, we proposed Ambiguity-aware Truncated Flow Matching (ATFM), addressing the challenge of jointly improving prediction accuracy and diversity via a novel inference paradigm and dedicated model components, tailored to the demands of AMIS tasks. Specifically, we firstly proposed Data-Hierarchical Inference, redefining a novel inference paradigm that disentangles prediction accuracy and diversity, which supervises a distribution for accuracy at 𝑇 trunc by marginalizing stochasticity and promoting diversity through controlled sampling in the following timesteps. Onthis foundation, we designed two key modules for ATFM: GTR, which explicitly models the Gaussian distribution at truncation point to ensure prediction fidelity and truncation distribution reliability for overall accuracy; and SFM, which extends semantic-aware flow transformation to model semantic consistency across predictions, annotations and intermediate states for enhancing prediction plausibility while promoting diversity. Experimental results on two public datasets showed that the proposed ATFM outperforms SOTA methods across all metrics and offers a more efficient inference process simultaneously. ATFM offers a versatile and reliable solution for AMIS across a broader range of scenarios through multifaceted analysis and outstanding performance.
240:Firstly, the overall inference paradigm is redefined through the proposed Data-Hierarchical Inference, which assigns distinct objectives to different stages. The truncation step focuses on enhancing overall accuracy at the distribution level without compromising diversity, while the subsequent diffusion stage promotes sample-level diversity based on the high-fidelity distribution obtained earlier. This overall design enables a principled disentanglement and simultaneous improvement of both accuracy and diversity. ( overall comparison between Fig. 7(b) and Fig. 7(c) )
246:Fig. 8 further analyzes the three main limitations of vanilla TDPMsin the context of AMIS requirements, along with the corresponding solutions introduced by ATFM, which fundamentally explains the reason behind the differences between the two inference paradigms and highlights the advantages of ATFM. Specifically, these limitations relate to the overall inference paradigm, the truncation process, and the post-truncation diffusion stage. In response, ATFM addresses these issues through the proposed Data-Hierarchical Inference, Gaussian Truncation Representation, and Semantic Flow Matching, respectively.
248:Figure 8: Limitations of vanilla TDPMs and the solutions offered by the proposed ATFM. From R1 to R3, they are the necessary requirements of performing reliable AMIS. From (a) to (c), they are the limitations of vanilla TDPMs, and from (d) to (f), they are the solutions and advantages of ATFM, respectively. (a) Vanilla TDPMs can't achieve simultaneous enhancement of accuracy and diversity due to the inherent trade-off. (b) Vanilla TDPMs lack prediction fidelity due to sub-optimal sampling-based truncation distribution approximation. (c) Vanilla TDPMs lack prediction plausibility due to the absence of semantic guidance. (d) Data-Hierarchical Inference in proposed ATFM introduces simultaneous enhancement of accuracy and diversity by a principled disentanglement. (e) GTR in proposed ATFM achieves high prediction fidelity via explicit Gaussian parameterization and modeling. (f) SFM in proposed ATFM achieves high prediction plausibility from semantic modeling in SFM.

1	## ATFM
     2	
     3	[AAAI-26 Oral] Official pytorch implementation of 'Ambiguity-aware Truncated Flow Matching for Ambiguous Medical Image Segmentation'
     4	
     5	
     6	## Introduction
     7	A simultaneous enhancement of accuracy and diversity of predictions remains a challenge in ambiguous medical image segmentation (AMIS) due to the inherent trade-offs. While truncated diffusion probabilistic models (TDPMs) hold strong potential with a paradigm optimization, existing TDPMs suffer from entangled accuracy and diversity of predictions with insufficient fidelity and plausibility. To address the aforementioned challenges, we propose Ambiguity-aware Truncated Flow Matching (ATFM), which introduces a novel inference paradigm and dedicated model components. Firstly, we propose Data-Hierarchical Inference, a redefinition of AMIS-specific inference paradigm, which enhances accuracy and diversity at data-distribution and data-sample level, respectively, for an effective disentanglement. Secondly, Gaussian Truncation Representation (GTR) is introduced to enhance both fidelity of predictions and reliability of truncation distribution, by explicitly modeling it as a Gaussian distribution at $T_{\text{trunc}}$ instead of using sampling-based approximations. Thirdly, Segmentation Flow Matching (SFM) is proposed to enhance the plausibility of diverse predictions by extending semantic-aware flow transformation in Flow Matching (FM). Comprehensive evaluations on LIDC and ISIC3 datasets demonstrate that ATFM outperforms SOTA methods and simultaneously achieves a more efficient inference. ATFM improves GED and HM-IoU by up to $12$% and $7.3$% compared to advanced methods.
     8	
     9	![Overview of proposed ATFM](/fig/main.png)
    10	
    11	## Requirements
    12	You can build the dependencies by executing the following command
    13	```
    14	conda create -n FM python=3.9
    15	source activate FM
    16	pip install -r requirement.txt
    17	```
    18	
    19	## Dataset
    20	Two public datasets: LIDC and ISIC Subset are implemented in this work. You can download the datasets from the following links:
    21	- [LIDC Dataset](https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1xKfKCQo8qa6SAr3u7qWNtQjIphIrvmd5) as preprocessed by @Stefan Knegt
    22	- [ISIC Subset](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1m7FdNldGqGyqw2L9GX8HDrHDId3kExtH/view?usp=sharing) as preprocessed by @killanzepf
    23	
    24	Please modify the dataset paths accordingly in `metadata_manager.py`
    25	
    26	## Training Procudure
    27	- Step 1: Train Gaussian Truncation Representation for both datasets
    28	  ```
    29	  python train_GTR.py --what LIDC --epochs 1000 --batchsize 256 --save_model True --save_model_step 50
    30	  python train_GTR.py --what isic3_style_concat --epochs 400 --batchsize 8 --save_model True --save_model_step 50
    31	  ```
    32	- Step 2: Train Segmentation Flow Matching based on the frozen GTR
    33	  ```
    34	  python train_prior_LIDC.py
    35	  python train_prior_ISIC.py
    36	  ```
    37	
    38	Note: The GPU memory consumption for ISIC Subset is `24198MiB (23.63GiB)` even with `batchsize=1`. `CUDA out-of-memory` errors may occur when too much memory is reserved. You may use `torch.utils.checkpoint` to reduce memory usage:
    39	```
    40	pred = model(image) # Original forward
    41	pred = torch.utils.checkpoint(model, image) # Forward with lower memory comsumption
    42	```
    43	
    44	## Testing Procedure
    45	```
    46	python test_prior_LIDC.py
    47	python test_prior_ISIC.py
    48	```
    49	
    50	Visualization of the predictions will show that ATFM produces a series of results with both high accuracy and high diversity, while fidelity and plausibility are simultaneously improved.
    51	![LIDC Dataset](/fig/pred.png)
    52	![ISIC Subset](/fig/pred1.png)
    53	
    54	## Acknowledgements
    55	- We thank [@killanzepf](https://github.com/kilianzepf/conditioned_uncertain_segmentation) for the preprocessed dataset and the GTR baseline.
    56	- We thank [@aleksandrinvictor](https://github.com/aleksandrinvictor/flow-matching) for the Flow Matching baseline.
    57	- We thank [@Stefan Knegt](https://github.com/stefanknegt/Probabilistic-Unet-Pytorch) for the preprocessed dataset.
    58	
    59	## Citations
    60	If you find this work helpful, please cite:
    61	```
    62	@inproceedings{li2026ambiguity,
    63	  title={Ambiguity-aware truncated flow matching for ambiguous medical image segmentation},
    64	  author={Li, Fanding and Li, Xiangyu and Su, Xianghe and Qiu, Xingyu and Dong, Suyu and Wang, Wei and Wang, Kuanquan and Luo, Gongning and Li, Shuo},
    65	  booktitle={Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence},
    66	  volume={40},
    67	  number={8},
    68	  pages={6082--6090},
    69	  year={2026}
    70	}
    71	```

1	import torch.nn as nn
     2	import torch
     3	import torch.distributions as td
     4	
     5	from utils.utils import *
     6	from models.unet import Unet
     7	
     8	
     9	class GaussianTruncationRepresentation(nn.Module):
    10	    def __init__(
    11	        self,
    12	        name,
    13	        num_channels=1,
    14	        num_classes=1,
    15	        num_filters=[32, 64, 128, 192],
    16	        rank: int = 10,
    17	        epsilon=1e-5,
    18	        diagonal=False,
    19	    ):
    20	        super().__init__()
    21	        self.name = name
    22	        self.rank = rank
    23	        self.num_channels = num_channels
    24	        self.num_classes = num_classes
    25	        self.epsilon = epsilon
    26	        conv_fn = nn.Conv2d
    27	        # whether to use only the diagonal (independent normals)
    28	        self.diagonal = diagonal
    29	        self.mean_l = conv_fn(num_filters[0], num_classes, kernel_size=1)
    30	        self.log_cov_diag_l = conv_fn(num_filters[0], num_classes, kernel_size=1)
    31	        self.cov_factor_l = conv_fn(num_filters[0], num_classes * rank, kernel_size=1)
    32	
    33	        self.unet = Unet(
    34	            name=self.name,
    35	            input_channels=self.num_channels,
    36	            num_classes=self.num_classes,
    37	            num_filters=[32, 64, 128, 192],
    38	            apply_last_layer=False,
    39	        )
    40	
    41	    def forward(self, image):
    42	        logits = self.unet.forward(image)
    43	        batch_size = logits.shape[0]  # Get the batchsize
    44	
    45	        # tensor size num_classesxHxW
    46	        event_shape = (self.num_classes,) + logits.shape[2:]
    47	
    48	        mean = self.mean_l(logits)
    49	        cov_diag = self.log_cov_diag_l(logits).exp() + self.epsilon
    50	        # Flattens out each image in the batch, size is batchsize x (rest)
    51	        mean = mean.view((batch_size, -1))
    52	        cov_diag = cov_diag.view((batch_size, -1))
    53	
    54	        cov_factor = self.cov_factor_l(logits)
    55	        cov_factor = cov_factor.view((batch_size, self.rank, self.num_classes, -1))
    56	        cov_factor = cov_factor.flatten(2, 3)
    57	        cov_factor = cov_factor.transpose(1, 2)
    58	
    59	        cov_factor = cov_factor
    60	        cov_diag = cov_diag + self.epsilon
    61	
    62	        # A dictionary that is handed over to the training loop for logging
    63	        infos_for_logging = {
    64	            "mean": mean,
    65	            "cov_factor": cov_factor,
    66	            "cov_diag": cov_diag,
    67	            "Max value of mean": torch.max(mean),
    68	            "Min value of mean": torch.min(mean),
    69	            "Max Value of Cov_diag": torch.max(cov_diag),
    70	            "Max Value of Cov_factor": torch.max(cov_factor),
    71	        }
    72	
    73	        if self.diagonal:
    74	            base_distribution = td.Independent(
    75	                td.Normal(loc=mean, scale=torch.sqrt(cov_diag)), 1
    76	            )
    77	        else:
    78	            try:
    79	                base_distribution = td.LowRankMultivariateNormal(
    80	                    loc=mean, cov_factor=cov_factor, cov_diag=cov_diag
    81	                )
    82	            except:
    83	                print(
    84	                    "Covariance became not invertible. Using independent normals for this batch!"
    85	                )
    86	                base_distribution = td.Independent(
    87	                    td.Normal(loc=mean, scale=torch.sqrt(cov_diag)), 1
    88	                )
    89	
    90	        distribution = ReshapedDistribution(
    91	            base_distribution=base_distribution,
    92	            new_event_shape=event_shape,
    93	            validate_args=False,
    94	        )
    95	
    96	        shape = (batch_size,) + event_shape
    97	        logit_mean = mean.view(shape)
    98	        cov_diag_view = cov_diag.view(shape).detach()
    99	        cov_factor_view = (
   100	            cov_factor.transpose(2, 1)
   101	            .view((batch_size, self.num_classes * self.rank) + event_shape[1:])
   102	            .detach()
   103	        )
   104	
   105	        output_dict = {
   106	            "logit_mean": logit_mean.detach(),
   107	            "cov_diag": cov_diag_view,
   108	            "cov_factor": cov_factor_view,
   109	            "distribution": distribution,
   110	        }
   111	
   112	        return logit_mean, output_dict, infos_for_logging

1	import torch
     2	from einops import rearrange
     3	
     4	
     5	class ConditionalFlow:
     6	    """Conditional Flow base class."""
     7	
     8	    def sample_p_t(self, x_0: torch.Tensor, x_1: torch.Tensor, t: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
     9	        """Samples points x_t conditioned on x_1.
    10	
    11	        Check eq. (10) in paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2210.02747
    12	
    13	        Args:
    14	            x_0: Samples from base distribution, [batch_size, 1, h, w].
    15	            x_1: Samples from target distribution, [batch_size, 1, h, w].
    16	            t: Time samples, [batch_size].
    17	
    18	        Returns:
    19	            Samples x_t from distribution p_t(x), [batch_size, 1, h, w].
    20	        """
    21	        raise NotImplementedError
    22	
    23	    def get_conditional_vector_field(self, x_0: torch.Tensor, x_1: torch.Tensor, t: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
    24	        """Counts conditional vector field based on samples from base and target distributions.
    25	
    26	        Check eq. (15) in paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2210.02747
    27	
    28	        Args:
    29	            x_0: Samples from base distribution, [batch_size, 1, h, w].
    30	            x_1: Samples from target distribution, [batch_size, 1, h, w].
    31	            t: Time samples, [batch_size].
    32	
    33	        Returns:
    34	            Target conditional vector field, [batch_size, 1, h, w].
    35	        """
    36	        raise NotImplementedError
    37	
    38	
    39	class OTConditionalFlow(ConditionalFlow):
    40	    def __init__(self, sigma_min) -> None:
    41	        """Inits optimal transport conditional flow object.
    42	
    43	        Args:
    44	            sigma_min: Minimal Gaussian distribution std for target distribution points.
    45	        """
    46	        self.sigma_min = sigma_min
    47	
    48	    def sample_p_t(self, x_0, x_1, t):
    49	        """Samples points x_t conditioned on x_1 using optimal transport path.
    50	
    51	        Check eq. (20) in paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2210.02747
    52	
    53	        Args:
    54	            x_0: Samples from base distribution, [batch_size, 1, h, w].
    55	            x_1: Samples from target distribution, [batch_size, 1, h, w].
    56	            t: Time samples, [batch_size].
    57	
    58	        Returns:
    59	            Samples x_t from distribution p_t(x).
    60	        """
    61	        t = rearrange(t, "b -> b 1 1 1")
    62	        mu_t = t * x_1
    63	        sigma_t = 1 - (1 - self.sigma_min) * t
    64	
    65	        x_t = mu_t + sigma_t * x_0
    66	
    67	        return x_t
    68	
    69	    def get_conditional_vector_field(self, x_0, x_1, t):
    70	        """Counts optimal transport conditional vector field.
    71	
    72	        Check eq. (23) in paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2210.02747
    73	
    74	        Args:
    75	            x_0: Samples from base distribution, [batch_size, 1, h, w].
    76	            x_1: Samples from target distribution, [batch_size, 1, h, w].
    77	            t: Time samples, [batch_size].
    78	
    79	        Returns:
    80	            Target optimal transport conditional vector field, [batch_size, 1, h, w].
    81	        """
    82	        return x_1 - (1 - self.sigma_min) * x_0

    30	def compute_hm_iou(preds_0, preds_1):
    31	    # Preds are assumed to be binary (foreground/background), reshaping them for comparison
    32	    preds_0 = preds_0.view(preds_0.shape[0], -1)
    33	    preds_1 = preds_1.view(preds_1.shape[0], -1)
    34	
    35	    # Calculate IoU matrix
    36	    intersection = (preds_0 & preds_1).float().sum(dim=1)
    37	    union = (preds_0 | preds_1).float().sum(dim=1)
    38	    iou = intersection / union
    39	
    40	    # Apply Hungarian Matching
    41	    cost_matrix = 1 - iou
    42	    _, col_indices = torch.linear_sum_assignment(cost_matrix)
    43	
    44	    # Calculate HM-IoU
    45	    hm_iou = (1 - iou[col_indices]).mean()
    46	
    47	    return hm_iou
    48	
    49	def Dice(target, predicted_mask):
    50	    target = target.detach()
    51	    predicted_mask = predicted_mask.detach()
    52	    smooth = 1e-8
    53	    true_p = (torch.logical_and(target == 1, predicted_mask == 1)).sum()
    54	    # true_n = (torch.logical_and(target == 0, predicted_mask == 0)).sum().item() #Currently not needed for Dice
    55	    Dice = (2. * true_p + smooth) / (torch.sum(target) + torch.sum(predicted_mask) + smooth)
    56	
    57	    return Dice
    58	
    59	def IoU(target, predicted_mask):
    60	    """
    61	    Args:
    62	        target: (torch.tensor (batchxCxHxW)) Binary Target Segmentation from training set
    63	        predicted_mask: (torch.tensor (batchxCxHxW)) Predicted Segmentation Mask
    64	
    65	    Returns:
    66	        IoU: (Float) Average IoUs over Batch
    67	    """
    68	    target = target.detach()
    69	    predicted_mask = predicted_mask.detach()
    70	    smooth = 1e-8
    71	    true_p = (torch.logical_and(target == 1, predicted_mask == 1)).sum()
    72	    # true_n = (torch.logical_and(target == 0, predicted_mask == 0)).sum().item() #Currently not needed for IoU
    73	    false_p = (torch.logical_and(target == 0, predicted_mask == 1)).sum()
    74	    false_n = (torch.logical_and(target == 1, predicted_mask == 0)).sum()
    75	    sample_IoU = (smooth+float(true_p))/(float(true_p) +
    76	                                         float(false_p)+float(false_n)+smooth)
    77	
    78	    return sample_IoU
    79	
    80	def loss_fn(
    81	    model,
    82	    target_flow,
    83	    x_0,
    84	    x_1,
    85	    t,
    86	    cond
    87	):
    88	    """Counts MSE loss between predicted and target conditional vector fields.
    89	
    90	    Check eq. (9) in paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2210.02747
    91	
    92	    Args:
    93	        model: Model that predicts conditional vector field.
    94	        target_flow: Object that models target conditional vector field.
    95	        x_0: Samples from base distribution, [batch_size, 1, h, w].
    96	        x_1: Samples from target distribution, [batch_size, 1, h, w].
    97	        t: Time samples, [batch_size].
    98	
    99	    Returns:
   100	        MSE loss between predicted and target conditional vector fields.
   101	    """
   102	    x_t = target_flow.sample_p_t(x_0=x_0, x_1=x_1, t=t).to(device)
   103	    predicted_cond_vector_field = model(x_t, t, cond)
   104	
   105	    X_1 = x_t + predicted_cond_vector_field * ((1. - t).view(t.shape[0], 1, 1, 1))
   106	    #print(X_1)
   107	    X_1 = X_1.ge(0.5)
   108	
   109	    target_cond_vector_field = target_flow.get_conditional_vector_field(x_0=x_0, x_1=x_1, t=t)
   110	
   111	    Loss1 = F.mse_loss(predicted_cond_vector_field, target_cond_vector_field)
   112	    Loss2 = Dice(X_1, x_1).mean()
   113	    Loss3 = IoU(X_1, x_1)
   114	    #Final Loss = Loss1 - alpha * Loss2
   115	    #print(f"Loss1:{Loss1}, Loss2:{Loss2}")
   116	
   117	    return Loss1, Loss2, Loss3
   118	
   119	
   120	def train(
   121	    prior_model,
   122	    model,
   123	    target_flow,
   124	    dataloader,
   125	    optimizer,
   126	    device,
   127	    num_epochs,

   131	    """Trains conditional vector field model.
   132	
   133	    Args:
   134	        model: Model that predicts conditional vector field.
   135	        target_flow: Object that models target conditional vector field.
   136	        dataloader: Dataloader.
   137	        optimizer: Optimizer.
   138	        device: Target device.
   139	        num_epochs: Num epochs to train.
   140	        save_path: Where to save checkpoints and intermediate results.
   141	    """
   142	    #base_distribution = Normal(0, 1)
   143	
   144	    for epoch in range(num_epochs - resume_epoch):
   145	        Loss = 0
   146	        Dice = 0
   147	        IoU = 0
   148	        prior_model.eval()
   149	        model.train()
   150	        print(f"Epochs:{epoch+1+resume_epoch}/{num_epochs} ... ")
   151	        print("Training")
   152	
   153	        for images, masks, _, _ in tqdm(dataloader):
   154	            back_loss = 0
   155	            optimizer.zero_grad()
   156	
   157	            images = images.to(device1)
   158	            logits, output_dict, _ = prior_model(images)
   159	            logit_distribution = output_dict["distribution"]
   160	
   161	            images = images.to(device)
   162	            #print(images.shape)
   163	            x_1 = masks[0]
   164	            batch_size = x_1.shape[0]
   165	            x_1 = x_1.to(device)
   166	            x_2 = masks[1].to(device)
   167	            x_3 = masks[2].to(device)
   168	            x_4 = masks[3].to(device)
   169	
   170	            x_0_1 = logit_distribution.sample()
   171	            x_0_1 = torch.sigmoid(x_0_1).to(device)
   172	
   173	            t = np.random.randint(low=0, high=1000, size=(batch_size,))
   174	            t = 1.0 * t / 1000
   175	            t = torch.tensor(t).float().to(device)
   176	
   177	            loss1, dice1, iou1 = loss_fn(model=model, target_flow=target_flow, x_0=x_0_1, x_1=x_1, t=t, cond=images)
   178	            loss2, dice2, iou2 = loss_fn(model=model, target_flow=target_flow, x_0=x_0_1, x_1=x_2, t=t, cond=images)
   179	            loss3, dice3, iou3 = loss_fn(model=model, target_flow=target_flow, x_0=x_0_1, x_1=x_4, t=t, cond=images)
   180	            loss4, dice4, iou4 = loss_fn(model=model, target_flow=target_flow, x_0=x_0_1, x_1=x_3, t=t, cond=images)
   181	            loss = 0.25 * (loss1 + loss2 + loss3 + loss4)
   182	            dice = 0.25 * (dice1 + dice2 + dice3 + dice4)
   183	            iou = 0.25 * (iou1 + iou2 + iou3 +iou4)
   184	            Loss += loss

   194	
   195	        Loss = Loss / (len(dataloader))

   198	        print(f'epoch:{epoch+1+resume_epoch}, Loss={Loss}, Dice={Dice}, IoU={IoU}')

    16	@torch.inference_mode()
    17	def infer(
    18	    model,
    19	    cond,
    20	    num_samples = 8,
    21	    num_steps = 100,
    22	    atol = 1e-4,
    23	    rtol = 1e-4,
    24	    method = "dopri5"
    25	):
    26	    """Generates images from model using numerical integration.
    27	
    28	    Args:
    29	        model: Model that predicts conditional vector field.
    30	        num_samples: How many samples to generate. Defaults to 16.
    31	        num_steps: Num steps for numerical integration. Defaults to 100.
    32	        atol: Upper bound on absolute error. Defaults to 1e-4.
    33	        rtol: Upper bound on relative error. Defaults to 1e-4.
    34	        method: Numerical integration method. Defaults to "dopri5".
    35	
    36	    Returns:
    37	        Predicted images on every timestamp, [num_steps, num_samples, 1, h, w]
    38	    """
    39	    model.eval()
    40	    device = next(model.parameters()).device
    41	
    42	    base_distribution = Normal(0, 1)
    43	    x_0 = base_distribution.sample(sample_shape=(num_samples*cond.shape[0], 1, 128, 128)).to(device)
    44	
    45	    timesteps = torch.linspace(0.0, 1.0, num_steps).to(device)
    46	    C = cond
    47	    for i in range(num_samples-1):
    48	        C = torch.cat((C, cond), dim = 0)
    49	    C = torch.reshape(C, ((num_samples*cond.shape[0], 1, 128, 128)))
    50	    #print(x_0.shape)
    51	    #print(timesteps.shape)
    52	    #print(C.shape)
    53	
    54	    output = odeint(
    55	        func=lambda t, x: model(x, t.repeat(num_samples*cond.shape[0]), C),
    56	        y0=x_0,
    57	        t=timesteps,
    58	        atol=atol,
    59	        rtol=rtol,
    60	        method=method,
    61	    )
    62	
    63	    return output

1	import math
     2	from functools import partial

     5	import torch
     6	import torch.nn.functional as F
     7	from einops import rearrange, reduce
     8	from einops.layers.torch import Rearrange
     9	from torch import einsum, nn
    10	from torch.utils.checkpoint import checkpoint
    11	
    12	
    13	# -------------------- Helpers --------------------
    14	def exists(x):
    15	    return x is not None
    16	
    17	
    18	def default(val, d):
    19	    if exists(val):
    20	        return val
    21	    return d() if isfunction(d) else d
    22	
    23	
    24	def num_to_groups(num, divisor):
    25	    groups = num // divisor
    26	    remainder = num % divisor
    27	    arr = [divisor] * groups
    28	    if remainder > 0:
    29	        arr.append(remainder)
    30	    return arr
    31	
    32	
    33	class Residual(nn.Module):
    34	    def __init__(self, fn):
    35	        super().__init__()
    36	        self.fn = fn
    37	
    38	    def forward(self, x, *args, **kwargs):
    39	        return self.fn(x, *args, **kwargs) + x
    40	
    41	
    42	def Upsample(dim, dim_out=None):
    43	    return nn.Sequential(
    44	        nn.Upsample(scale_factor=2, mode="nearest"),
    45	        nn.Conv2d(dim, default(dim_out, dim), 3, padding=1),
    46	    )
    47	
    48	
    49	def Downsample(dim, dim_out=None):
    50	    # No More Strided Convolutions or Pooling
    51	    return nn.Sequential(
    52	        Rearrange("b c (h p1) (w p2) -> b (c p1 p2) h w", p1=2, p2=2),
    53	        nn.Conv2d(dim * 4, default(dim_out, dim), 1),
    54	    )
    55	
    56	
    57	# -------------------- Positional embeddings --------------------
    58	class SinusoidalPositionEmbeddings(nn.Module):
    59	    def __init__(self, dim):
    60	        super().__init__()
    61	        self.dim = dim
    62	
    63	    def forward(self, time):
    64	        device = time.device
    65	        half_dim = self.dim // 2
    66	        embeddings = math.log(10000) / (half_dim - 1)
    67	        embeddings = torch.exp(torch.arange(half_dim, device=device) * -embeddings)
    68	        embeddings = time[:, None] * embeddings[None, :]
    69	        embeddings = torch.cat((embeddings.sin(), embeddings.cos()), dim=-1)
    70	
    71	        return embeddings
    72	
    73	
    74	# -------------------- Resnet block --------------------
    75	class WeightStandardizedConv2d(nn.Conv2d):
    76	    """
    77	    https://arxiv.org/abs/1903.10520
    78	    weight standardization purportedly works synergistically with group normalization
    79	    """
    80	
    81	    def forward(self, x):
    82	        eps = 1e-5 if x.dtype == torch.float32 else 1e-3
    83	
    84	        weight = self.weight
    85	        mean = reduce(weight, "o ... -> o 1 1 1", "mean")
    86	        var = reduce(weight, "o ... -> o 1 1 1", partial(torch.var, unbiased=False))
    87	        normalized_weight = (weight - mean) / (var + eps).rsqrt()
    88	
    89	        return F.conv2d(
    90	            x,
    91	            normalized_weight,
    92	            self.bias,
    93	            self.stride,
    94	            self.padding,
    95	            self.dilation,
    96	            self.groups,
    97	        )
    98	
    99	
   100	class Block(nn.Module):
   101	    def __init__(self, dim, dim_out, groups=8):
   102	        super().__init__()
   103	        self.proj = WeightStandardizedConv2d(dim, dim_out, 3, padding=1)
   104	        self.norm = nn.GroupNorm(groups, dim_out)
   105	        self.act = nn.SiLU()
   106	
   107	    def forward(self, x, scale_shift=None):
   108	        x = self.proj(x)
   109	        x = self.norm(x)
   110	
   111	        if exists(scale_shift):
   112	            scale, shift = scale_shift
   113	            x = x * (scale + 1) + shift
   114	
   115	        x = self.act(x)
   116	        return x
   117	
   118	
   119	class ResnetBlock(nn.Module):
   120	    """https://arxiv.org/abs/1512.03385"""
   121	
   122	    def __init__(self, dim, dim_out, *, time_emb_dim=None, groups=8):
   123	        super().__init__()
   124	        self.mlp = nn.Sequential(nn.SiLU(), nn.Linear(time_emb_dim, dim_out * 2)) if exists(time_emb_dim) else None
   125	
   126	        self.block1 = Block(dim, dim_out, groups=groups)
   127	        self.block2 = Block(dim_out, dim_out, groups=groups)
   128	        self.res_conv = nn.Conv2d(dim, dim_out, 1) if dim != dim_out else nn.Identity()
   129	
   130	    def forward(self, x, time_emb=None):
   131	        scale_shift = None
   132	        if exists(self.mlp) and exists(time_emb):
   133	            time_emb = self.mlp(time_emb)
   134	            time_emb = rearrange(time_emb, "b c -> b c 1 1")
   135	            scale_shift = time_emb.chunk(2, dim=1)
   136	
   137	        h = self.block1(x, scale_shift=scale_shift)
   138	        h = self.block2(h)
   139	        return h + self.res_conv(x)
   140	
   141	
   142	# -------------------- Attention module --------------------
   143	class Attention(nn.Module):
   144	    def __init__(self, dim, heads=4, dim_head=32):
   145	        super().__init__()
   146	        self.scale = dim_head**-0.5
   147	        self.heads = heads
   148	        hidden_dim = dim_head * heads
   149	        self.to_qkv = nn.Conv2d(dim, hidden_dim * 3, 1, bias=False)
   150	        self.to_out = nn.Conv2d(hidden_dim, dim, 1)
   151	
   152	    def forward(self, x):
   153	        b, c, h, w = x.shape
   154	        qkv = self.to_qkv(x).chunk(3, dim=1)
   155	        q, k, v = map(lambda t: rearrange(t, "b (h c) x y -> b h c (x y)", h=self.heads), qkv)
   156	        q = q * self.scale
   157	
   158	        sim = einsum("b h d i, b h d j -> b h i j", q, k)
   159	        sim = sim - sim.amax(dim=-1, keepdim=True).detach()
   160	        attn = sim.softmax(dim=-1)
   161	
   162	        out = einsum("b h i j, b h d j -> b h i d", attn, v)
   163	        out = rearrange(out, "b h (x y) d -> b (h d) x y", x=h, y=w)
   164	        return self.to_out(out)
   165	
   166	
   167	class LinearAttention(nn.Module):
   168	    def __init__(self, dim, heads=4, dim_head=32):
   169	        super().__init__()
   170	        self.scale = dim_head**-0.5
   171	        self.heads = heads
   172	        hidden_dim = dim_head * heads
   173	        self.to_qkv = nn.Conv2d(dim, hidden_dim * 3, 1, bias=False)
   174	
   175	        self.to_out = nn.Sequential(nn.Conv2d(hidden_dim, dim, 1), nn.GroupNorm(1, dim))
   176	
   177	    def forward(self, x):
   178	        b, c, h, w = x.shape
   179	        qkv = self.to_qkv(x).chunk(3, dim=1)
   180	        q, k, v = map(lambda t: rearrange(t, "b (h c) x y -> b h c (x y)", h=self.heads), qkv)
   181	
   182	        q = q.softmax(dim=-2)
   183	        k = k.softmax(dim=-1)
   184	
   185	        q = q * self.scale
   186	        context = torch.einsum("b h d n, b h e n -> b h d e", k, v)
   187	
   188	        out = torch.einsum("b h d e, b h d n -> b h e n", context, q)
   189	        out = rearrange(out, "b h c (x y) -> b (h c) x y", h=self.heads, x=h, y=w)
   190	        return self.to_out(out)
   191	
   192	
   193	# -------------------- Group normalization --------------------
   194	class PreNorm(nn.Module):
   195	    def __init__(self, dim, fn):
   196	        super().__init__()
   197	        self.fn = fn
   198	        self.norm = nn.GroupNorm(1, dim)
   199	
   200	    def forward(self, x):
   201	        x = self.norm(x)
   202	        return self.fn(x)
   203	
   204	
   205	# -------------------- Unet --------------------
   206	class Unet(nn.Module):
   207	    def __init__(
   208	        self,
   209	        dim,
   210	        init_dim=None,
   211	        out_dim=None,
   212	        dim_mults=(1, 2, 4, 8),
   213	        channels=1,
   214	        self_condition=True,
   215	        resnet_block_groups=4,
   216	    ):
   217	        super().__init__()
   218	
   219	        # determine dimensions
   220	        self.channels = channels
   221	        self.self_condition = self_condition
   222	        input_channels = channels * (2 if self_condition else 1)
   223	
   224	        init_dim = default(init_dim, dim)
   225	        self.init_conv = nn.Conv2d(input_channels, init_dim, 1, padding=0)  # changed to 1 and 0 from 7,3
   226	
   227	        dims = [init_dim, *map(lambda m: dim * m, dim_mults)]
   228	        in_out = list(zip(dims[:-1], dims[1:]))
   229	
   230	        block_klass = partial(ResnetBlock, groups=resnet_block_groups)
   231	
   232	        # time embeddings
   233	        time_dim = dim * 4
   234	
   235	        self.time_mlp = nn.Sequential(
   236	            SinusoidalPositionEmbeddings(dim),
   237	            nn.Linear(dim, time_dim),
   238	            nn.GELU(),
   239	            nn.Linear(time_dim, time_dim),
   240	        )
   241	
   242	        # layers
   243	        self.downs = nn.ModuleList([])
   244	        self.ups = nn.ModuleList([])
   245	        num_resolutions = len(in_out)
   246	
   247	        for ind, (dim_in, dim_out) in enumerate(in_out):
   248	            is_last = ind >= (num_resolutions - 1)
   249	
   250	            self.downs.append(
   251	                nn.ModuleList(
   252	                    [
   253	                        block_klass(dim_in, dim_in, time_emb_dim=time_dim),
   254	                        block_klass(dim_in, dim_in, time_emb_dim=time_dim),
   255	                        Residual(PreNorm(dim_in, LinearAttention(dim_in))),
   256	                        Downsample(dim_in, dim_out) if not is_last else nn.Conv2d(dim_in, dim_out, 3, padding=1),
   257	                    ]
   258	                )
   259	            )
   260	
   261	        mid_dim = dims[-1]
   262	        self.mid_block1 = block_klass(mid_dim, mid_dim, time_emb_dim=time_dim)
   263	        self.mid_attn = Residual(PreNorm(mid_dim, Attention(mid_dim)))
   264	        self.mid_block2 = block_klass(mid_dim, mid_dim, time_emb_dim=time_dim)
   265	
   266	        for ind, (dim_in, dim_out) in enumerate(reversed(in_out)):
   267	            is_last = ind == (len(in_out) - 1)
   268	
   269	            self.ups.append(
   270	                nn.ModuleList(
   271	                    [
   272	                        block_klass(dim_out + dim_in, dim_out, time_emb_dim=time_dim),
   273	                        block_klass(dim_out + dim_in, dim_out, time_emb_dim=time_dim),
   274	                        Residual(PreNorm(dim_out, LinearAttention(dim_out))),
   275	                        Upsample(dim_out, dim_in) if not is_last else nn.Conv2d(dim_out, dim_in, 3, padding=1),
   276	                    ]
   277	                )
   278	            )
   279	
   280	        self.out_dim = default(out_dim, channels)
   281	
   282	        self.final_res_block = block_klass(dim * 2, dim, time_emb_dim=time_dim)
   283	        self.final_conv = nn.Conv2d(dim, self.out_dim, 1)
   284	
   285	    def forward(self, x, time, x_self_cond):
   286	        if self.self_condition:
   287	            x_self_cond = default(x_self_cond, lambda: torch.zeros_like(x))
   288	            x = torch.cat((x_self_cond, x), dim=1)
   289	
   290	        x = self.init_conv(x)
   291	        r = x.clone()
   292	
   293	        t = self.time_mlp(time)
   294	
   295	        h = []
   296	
   297	        for block1, block2, attn, downsample in self.downs:
   298	            x = block1(x, t)
   299	            h.append(x)
   300	
   301	            x = block2(x, t)
   302	            x = attn(x)
   303	            h.append(x)
   304	
   305	            x = downsample(x)
   306	
   307	        x = self.mid_block1(x, t)
   308	        x = self.mid_attn(x)
   309	        x = self.mid_block2(x, t)
   310	
   311	        for block1, block2, attn, upsample in self.ups:
   312	            x = torch.cat((x, h.pop()), dim=1)
   313	            x = block1(x, t)
   314	
   315	            x = torch.cat((x, h.pop()), dim=1)
   316	            x = block2(x, t)
   317	            x = attn(x)
   318	
   319	            x = upsample(x)
   320	
   321	        x = torch.cat((x, r), dim=1)
   322	
   323	        x = self.final_res_block(x, t)
   324	        #x = checkpoint(self.final_res_block, x, t)  if cuda-out-of-memory
   325	        return self.final_conv(x)

13:A simultaneous enhancement of accuracy and diversity of predictions remains a challenge in ambiguous medical image segmentation (AMIS) due to the inherent trade-offs. While truncated diffusion probabilistic models (TDPMs) hold strong potential with a paradigm optimization, existing TDPMs suffer from entangled accuracy and diversity of predictions with insufficient fidelity and plausibility. To address the aforementioned challenges, we propose Ambiguity-aware Truncated Flow Matching (ATFM), which introduces a novel inference paradigm and dedicated model components. Firstly, we propose Data-Hierarchical Inference, a redefinition of AMISspecific inference paradigm, which enhances accuracy and diversity at data-distribution and data-sample level, respectively, for an effective disentanglement. Secondly, Gaussian Truncation Representation (GTR) is introduced to enhance both fidelity of predictions and reliability of truncation distribution, by explicitly modeling it as a Gaussian distribution at 𝑇 trunc instead of using sampling-based approximations. Thirdly, Segmentation Flow Matching (SFM) is proposed to enhance the plausibility of diverse predictions by extending semantic-aware flow transformation in Flow Matching (FM). Comprehensive evaluations on LIDC and ISIC3 datasets demonstrate that ATFM outperforms SOTA methods and simultaneously achieves a more efficient inference. ATFM improves GED and HM-IoU by up to 12% and 7 . 3% compared to advanced methods.
37:In this work, we propose the Ambiguity-aware Truncated Flow Matching (ATFM) to achieve a synergistic enhancement of accuracy and diversity of predictions in AMIS tasks, supported by three designs (as Fig. 1(b)): (1) We propose Data-Hierarchical Inference, an innovative AMIS-specific inference paradigm redefinition inspired by TDPMs, where stochasticity during diffusion is marginalized for an effective disentanglement of accuracy and diversity. Specifically, ATFMperforms truncated steps at the data distribution level to prioritize accuracy with diversity marginalized, whereas the final diffusion stage operates at the data sample level to enhance diversity without sacrificing accuracy. (2) We propose Gaussian Truncation Representation (GTR), which explicitly models the Gaussian latent distribution at truncation point to enhance prediction fidelity. While traditional TDPMs approximate the implicit distribution via adversarial sampling, GTR encodes image-level semantic features to logit distribution, thereby directly modeling the reliable distribution at the truncation point. This design preserves low-frequency modes and improves consistency, leading to predictions with higher fidelity in AMIS tasks. (3) We propose Segmentation Flow Matching (SFM), which introduces semantic-aware flow transformation to increase diversity and enhance plausibility simultaneously. While traditional TDPMs focus solely on generative quality after truncated steps, the proposed SFM
100:## Gaussian Truncation Representation Improves Fidelity via Explicit Gaussian Modeling
242:Secondly, the truncation stage is redefined via the proposed Gaussian Truncation Representation (GTR), which explicitly models the truncation point as a learnable Gaussian distribution with parameterized mean and covariance. This replaces the sampling-based approximation in TDPMs with a stable and semantically meaningful representation. As a result, it enhances prediction fidelity and provides a reliable initialization for subsequent inference. ( comparison illustrated in green boxes between Fig. 7(b) and Fig. 7(c) )
246:Fig. 8 further analyzes the three main limitations of vanilla TDPMsin the context of AMIS requirements, along with the corresponding solutions introduced by ATFM, which fundamentally explains the reason behind the differences between the two inference paradigms and highlights the advantages of ATFM. Specifically, these limitations relate to the overall inference paradigm, the truncation process, and the post-truncation diffusion stage. In response, ATFM addresses these issues through the proposed Data-Hierarchical Inference, Gaussian Truncation Representation, and Semantic Flow Matching, respectively.

To address the aforementioned challenges, we propose Ambiguity-aware Truncated Flow Matching (ATFM), which introduces a novel inference paradigm and dedicated model components. Firstly, we propose Data-Hierarchical Inference, a redefinition of AMISspecific inference paradigm, which enhances accuracy and diversity at data-distribution and data-sample level, respectively, for an effective disentanglement. Secondly, Gaussian Truncation Representation (GTR) is introduced to enhance both fidelity of predictions and reliability of truncation distribution, by explicitly modeling it as a Gaussian distribution at 𝑇 trunc instead of using sampling-based approximations. Thirdly, Segmentation Flow Matching (SFM) is proposed to enhance the plausibility of diverse predictions by extending semantic-aware flow transformation in Flow Matching (FM). Comprehensive evaluations on LIDC and ISIC3 datasets demonstrate that ATFM outperforms SOTA methods and simultaneously achieves a more efficient inference. ATFM improves GED and HM-IoU by up to 12% and 7 . 3% compared to advanced methods.
37:In this work, we propose the Ambiguity-aware Truncated Flow Matching (ATFM) to achieve a synergistic enhancement of accuracy and diversity of predictions in AMIS tasks, supported by three designs (as Fig. 1(b)): (1) We propose Data-Hierarchical Inference, an innovative AMIS-specific inference paradigm redefinition inspired by TDPMs, where stochasticity during diffusion is marginalized for an effective disentanglement of accuracy and diversity. Specifically, ATFMperforms truncated steps at the data distribution level to prioritize accuracy with diversity marginalized, whereas the final diffusion stage operates at the data sample level to enhance diversity without sacrificing accuracy. (2) We propose Gaussian Truncation Representation (GTR), which explicitly models the Gaussian latent distribution at truncation point to enhance prediction fidelity. While traditional TDPMs approximate the implicit distribution via adversarial sampling, GTR encodes image-level semantic features to logit distribution, thereby directly modeling the reliable distribution at the truncation point. This design preserves low-frequency modes and improves consistency, leading to predictions with higher fidelity in AMIS tasks. (3) We propose Segmentation Flow Matching (SFM), which introduces semantic-aware flow transformation to increase diversity and enhance plausibility simultaneously.
